session_title: Why Electronics Run the Car — E/E Architecture & ECU Concepts

questions.question: What is the fundamental pattern that every electronic system in a modern vehicle follows?
questions.explanation: Every electronic vehicle system follows the sensor-ECU-actuator pattern: a sensor measures a physical quantity, the ECU processes the data and makes a decision, and the actuator performs a physical action. Option A describes the starting/charging system components, not a universal control pattern.

questions.question: What does the abbreviation ECM stand for in automotive electronics?
questions.explanation: ECM stands for Engine Control Module, the most complex and critical ECU in any internal combustion engine vehicle. It controls fuel injection, ignition timing, idle speed, and emissions systems. Option A is a fabricated term not used in automotive terminology.

questions.question: Why was CAN bus developed as an in-vehicle network?

choices.A: To allow ECUs to share a communication bus instead of requiring point-to-point wiring between each pair
choices.B: To increase the voltage capacity of vehicle wiring
choices.C: To replace fuses and relays throughout the vehicle
choices.D: To provide backup power when the battery fails

questions.explanation: CAN bus solved the wiring problem: with 100 ECUs, point-to-point wiring would require nearly 5,000 individual connections. CAN allows all ECUs to share two wires (CAN-High and CAN-Low) and broadcast messages with unique IDs. Option C is incorrect because CAN is a communication network, not a replacement for circuit protection devices.

questions.question: Which ECU is responsible for controlling anti-lock braking, traction control, and electronic stability control?
questions.explanation: The ABS/ESC module (also called the hydraulic control unit) individually modulates brake pressure at each wheel to provide anti-lock braking, traction control, and stability control. The BCM (option A) handles comfort and convenience functions like lighting, locking, and wipers.

questions.question: In the OBD-II standard, what does the abbreviation DTC stand for?
questions.explanation: DTC stands for Diagnostic Trouble Code, a standardised fault code stored by an ECU when it detects a malfunction. Technicians read DTCs using a scan tool connected to the OBD-II port. Option C is fabricated and not an automotive diagnostic term.

questions.question: A technician plugs a scan tool into a vehicle and finds DTC P0117 — 'Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Input.' Based on the sensor-ECU-actuator model, which part of the pattern has likely failed?

choices.B: The ECU itself has a software corruption
choices.C: The sensor or its wiring to the ECU has a fault
choices.D: The CAN bus termination resistor is missing

questions.explanation: The DTC describes a sensor circuit low input, pointing to a problem with the coolant temperature sensor or its wiring (open circuit, short to ground, or damaged sensor). In the sensor-ECU-actuator pattern, a 'circuit low input' code identifies the input (sensor) side. Option A describes an actuator problem that would generate a different type of fault code.
questions.topic: Applying the sensor-ECU-actuator pattern to diagnostics

questions.question: A vehicle has a fuel pump that will not turn on. The technician confirms 12V is present at the fuel pump relay coil terminals, but the relay does not click. Using the sensor-ECU-actuator model, which sequence correctly describes the fuel pump control path?

choices.A: Fuel pump sensor reads fuel pressure, BCM commands the relay, relay powers the pump
choices.B: Ignition switch triggers the ECM, ECM grounds the relay coil, relay contacts power the fuel pump
choices.C: The fuel pump is directly connected to the battery through a fuse with no ECU involvement
choices.D: The CAN bus sends a message to the fuel pump motor directly

questions.explanation: The ECM controls the fuel pump relay by providing a ground path to the relay coil. When the ECM receives the ignition-on signal, it grounds pin 86, energising the coil and closing the contacts that power the fuel pump. Option C is incorrect because on modern vehicles, the ECM controls when the fuel pump runs, not a simple direct connection.
questions.topic: ECU control paths and relay operation

questions.question: A premium vehicle has 120 ECUs. If these were connected point-to-point instead of using a CAN bus, approximately how many individual wire connections would be needed?
questions.explanation: Point-to-point connections between n nodes requires n(n-1)/2 connections. For 120 ECUs: 120 x 119 / 2 = 7,140. This enormous number illustrates why shared network buses like CAN were essential. Option A simply doubles the ECU count and does not account for the combinatorial nature of point-to-point wiring.
questions.topic: CAN bus and the wiring problem

questions.question: A technician reads live data from the OBD-II scan tool and observes that the coolant temperature PID reads -40 degrees C even though the engine is warm. The engine is running rough and using excessive fuel. Which analysis is most likely correct?

choices.A: The alternator is overcharging, causing false sensor readings across all systems
choices.B: The coolant temperature sensor circuit has an open circuit, the ECU defaults to -40 degrees C, and enriches the fuel mixture as if the engine were extremely cold
choices.C: The OBD-II scan tool is faulty and displaying incorrect values
choices.D: The thermostat is stuck open, causing the engine to actually run at -40 degrees C

questions.explanation: When a temperature sensor circuit is open, many ECMs default to -40 degrees C (the bottom of the sensor range). The ECM then commands a rich fuel mixture appropriate for extreme cold, causing rough running and high fuel consumption. Option D is physically impossible since an engine generates heat and cannot reach -40 degrees C while running.
questions.topic: Interpreting live data and sensor faults

questions.question: A vehicle's instrument cluster shows the correct speed and RPM, but the transmission shifts erratically. A scan tool reveals no DTCs in the ECM but shows a communication fault in the TCU. Based on the vehicle network architecture, what is the most likely cause?

choices.A: The CAN bus connection to the TCU is faulty while the rest of the powertrain CAN network is intact
choices.B: The instrument cluster is consuming too much CAN bus bandwidth
choices.C: The OBD-II port has a broken pin preventing communication with all ECUs
choices.D: The battery is too weak to power the TCU

questions.explanation: Since the ECM and instrument cluster are communicating normally (correct speed and RPM display), the powertrain CAN bus backbone is functional. A localised CAN bus fault at the TCU connection (damaged wiring, corroded connector, or faulty CAN transceiver in the TCU) would isolate only the TCU. Option C is contradicted by the fact that the scan tool successfully communicated with the ECM.
questions.topic: CAN bus fault isolation and network diagnostics
